import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

def task_func(list_of_pairs):
    # Create a DataFrame from the list of pairs
    df = pd.DataFrame(list_of_pairs, columns=['Category', 'Value'])
    
    # Create a barplot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', data=df, ax=ax)
    
    # Set the title of the barplot
    ax.set_title('Category vs Value')
    
    # Show the plot
    plt.show()
    
    return df, ax

    expected_title = 'Category vs Value'
    return ax.get_title() == expected_title

import ast
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(csv_file):
    # Read the CSV file into a DataFrame
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_file)

    # Convert the string representations of dictionaries in 'dict_column' to actual dictionaries
    df['dict_column'] = df['dict_column'].apply(ast.literal_eval)

    # If you want to expand the dictionary into separate columns, you can uncomment the following line:
    # dict_df = df['dict_column'].apply(pd.Series)
    # df = df.join(dict_df).drop('dict_column', axis=1)

    # Create a pairplot for visualization
    ax = sns.pairplot(df)

    plt.show()  # To display the plot (optional)
    
    return df, ax

import nltk
from string import punctuation
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# Constants
PUNCTUATION = set(punctuation)

def task_func(text):
    words = text.split()
    dollar_words = [word for word in words if word.startswith('$') and any(c.isalnum() for c in word[1:])]
    
    if not dollar_words:
        return (None, False)
    
    freq_dict = {}
    for word in dollar_words:
        freq_dict[word] = freq_dict.get(word, 0) + 1
    
    # Create a bar plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    ax = sns.barplot(x=list(freq_dict.keys()), y=list(freq_dict.values()))
    ax.set_xlabel('Words')
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    ax.set_title('Frequency of Words Starting with "$"')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    return (ax, True)

Ground Truth Function:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df, target_column):
    # Separate features and target
    X = df.drop(columns=[target_column])
    y = df[target_column]
    
    # Train the Random Forest Classifier
    clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    clf.fit(X, y)
    
    # Get feature importances
    importances = clf.feature_importances_
    feature_names = X.columns
    
    # Create a DataFrame for feature importance
    feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'Feature': feature_names,
        'Importance': importances
    }).sort_values(by='Importance', ascending=False)
    
    # Plotting the bar plot of feature importance
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    axes = sns.barplot(x='Importance', y='Feature', data=feature_importance_df)
    axes.set_xlabel('Feature Importance Score')
    axes.set_ylabel('Features')
    axes.set_title('Visualizing Important Features')
    plt.show()
    
    return clf, axes

def plot_correctness(output, ground_truth):
    axes = output[1]  # extract the axes from tuple
    importance_scores = axes.containers[0].datavalues  # get the feature importance scores from the bar plot
    feature_names = [bar.get_x() for bar in axes.containers[0]]  # get feature names from bars
    importance_dict = dict(zip(feature_names, importance_scores))
    
    # Check if the bar plot sorted correctly
    sorted_importance = sorted(importance_dict.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    
    return list(importance_dict.items()) == sorted_importance  # check if the plot corresponds to the sorted importance

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Constants
FEATURE_NAMES = ["Feature 1", "Feature 2", "Feature 3", "Feature 4", "Feature 5"]

def task_func(data_matrix):
    # Standardize the data
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    std_data_array = scaler.fit_transform(data_matrix)
    
    # Calculate mean of each row
    mean_values = std_data_array.mean(axis=1)
    
    # Create a DataFrame with standardized data and mean values
    std_data = pd.DataFrame(std_data_array, columns=FEATURE_NAMES)
    std_data['Mean'] = mean_values
    
    # Visualize the distribution of the mean values
    fig, hist_ax = plt.subplots()
    hist_ax.hist(mean_values, bins='auto', alpha=0.7, color='blue')
    hist_ax.set_title('Distribution of Means')
    hist_ax.set_xlabel('Mean Value')
    hist_ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    # Show the plot
    plt.show()
    
    return std_data, hist_ax

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
from scipy.stats import zscore
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(data_matrix):
    # Calculate Z-scores for the data matrix
    z_scores = zscore(data_matrix, axis=1)
    z_scores_df = pd.DataFrame(z_scores, columns=[f'Feature {i+1}' for i in range(z_scores.shape[1])])
    
    # Calculate mean of Z-scores for each row
    z_scores_df['Mean'] = z_scores_df.mean(axis=1)
    
    # Visualize the correlation matrix with a heatmap
    correlation_matrix = z_scores_df.corr()
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
    heatmap_ax = sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f", square=True, linewidths=.5)
    plt.title('Correlation Matrix of Z-scores')
    
    return (z_scores_df, heatmap_ax)

import pandas as pd

from scipy.stats import skew

def task_func(data_matrix):
    # Convert the input data_matrix to a DataFrame
    df = pd.DataFrame(data_matrix)
    
    # Calculate skewness for each row
    df['Skewness'] = df.apply(skew, axis=1)
    
    # Plot the distribution of skewness values
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    df['Skewness'].hist(ax=ax, bins=10)
    ax.set_title('Distribution of Skewness')
    ax.set_xlabel('Skewness')
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    return (df[['Skewness']], ax)

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

def task_func(data_matrix, n_components=2):
    # Initialize PCA
    pca = PCA(n_components=n_components)
    
    # Fit and transform the data
    pca_transformed = pca.fit_transform(data_matrix)
    
    # Calculate the mean value of each component
    mean_values = pca_transformed.mean(axis=0)
    
    # Create a DataFrame with the PCA results
    pca_df = pd.DataFrame(pca_transformed, columns=[f'Component {i+1}' for i in range(n_components)])
    
    # Add a column for the mean value of the components per row
    pca_df['Mean'] = pca_df.mean(axis=1)
    
    # Calculate cumulative explained variance
    cum_var = pca.explained_variance_ratio_.cumsum()
    
    # Plot cumulative explained variance
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(range(1, n_components + 1), cum_var, marker='o')
    ax.set_xlabel('Number of Components')
    ax.set_ylabel('Cumulative Explained Variance')
    ax.set_title('Cumulative Explained Variance by PCA Components')
    
    # Return the DataFrame and the plot
    return pca_df, ax

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.axes import Axes
from typing import List, Tuple

def task_func(df: pd.DataFrame) -> Tuple[pd.DataFrame, List[Axes]]:
    # Replace NaN values with the mean of each numeric column
    df.fillna(df.mean(), inplace=True)

    # Calculate statistics
    stats_df = df.describe()

    # Create distribution plots
    dist_plots = []
    numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns

    plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))
    for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
        plt.subplot(1, len(numeric_cols), i + 1)
        ax = sns.histplot(df[col], bins=10, kde=True)
        ax.set_title(f'Distribution of {col}')
        dist_plots.append(ax)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    return stats_df, dist_plots

from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

def task_func(df):
    # Replace missing values with column's average
    df.fillna(df.mean(), inplace=True)
    
    # Normalize numeric columns
    scaler = MinMaxScaler()
    norm_data = scaler.fit_transform(df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int']))
    norm_df = pd.DataFrame(norm_data, columns=df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int']).columns)

    # Draw box plots for each normalized column
    boxplot_axes = norm_df.plot.box(figsize=(10, 6), return_type='axes')
    plt.title('Box Plots of Normalized Numeric Columns')
    plt.tight_layout()

    return norm_df, boxplot_axes

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df: pd.DataFrame):
    # Exclude non-numeric columns
    numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number])
    
    # Replace missing values with the column's average
    numeric_df.fillna(numeric_df.mean(), inplace=True)

    # Perform PCA
    pca = PCA(n_components=2)
    components = pca.fit_transform(numeric_df)

    # Create a new DataFrame for the principal components
    pca_df = pd.DataFrame(data=components, columns=['Component 1', 'Component 2'])

    # Create a scatter plot
    fig, scatter_ax = plt.subplots()
    scatter_ax.scatter(pca_df['Component 1'], pca_df['Component 2'])
    scatter_ax.set_xlabel('Component 1')
    scatter_ax.set_ylabel('Component 2')
    scatter_ax.set_title('PCA of DataFrame')

    plt.show()

    return (pca_df, scatter_ax)

def variance_ratio(output, ground_truth):
    from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
    pca_df_output = output[0]
    pca_df_ground_truth = ground_truth[0]

    pca_output = PCA(n_components=2)
    pca_output.fit(pca_df_output.dropna())
    explained_variance_output = pca_output.explained_variance_ratio_

    pca_ground_truth = PCA(n_components=2)
    pca_ground_truth.fit(pca_df_ground_truth.dropna())
    explained_variance_ground_truth = pca_ground_truth.explained_variance_ratio_

    cosine_sim = cosine_similarity([explained_variance_output], [explained_variance_ground_truth])[0][0]

    normalized_cosine_sim = (cosine_sim + 1) / 2

    return normalized_cosine_sim

from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

def task_func(df):
    # Fill missing values with the column's average
    df.fillna(df.mean(), inplace=True)
    
    # Standardize numeric columns
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns
    df[numeric_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(df[numeric_cols])
    
    # Calculate the correlation matrix
    correlation_matrix = df.corr()
    
    # Create a heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
    heatmap_axes = sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap='coolwarm', square=True)
    
    # Return standardized DataFrame and heatmap axes
    return (df, heatmap_axes)

from datetime import datetime
import pandas as pd
import pytz
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Constants
DATE_FORMAT = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"
TIMEZONES = [
    "America/New_York",
    "Europe/London",
    "Asia/Shanghai",
    "Asia/Tokyo",
    "Australia/Sydney",
]

def task_func(timestamp):
    datetimes = []
    for tz in TIMEZONES:
        timezone = pytz.timezone(tz)
        local_time = datetime.fromtimestamp(timestamp, timezone).strftime(DATE_FORMAT)
        datetimes.append((tz, local_time))

    df = pd.DataFrame(datetimes, columns=["Timezone", "Datetime"])
    df['Datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Datetime'], format=DATE_FORMAT)

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    df.set_index('Timezone')['Datetime'].plot(kind='bar', ax=ax)
    
    ax.set_xlabel('Timezone')
    ax.set_ylabel('Datetime')
    ax.set_title(f'Datetime = f(Timezone)')
    
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    return df, ax

import pandas as pd
import regex as re
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer

def task_func(text):
    sentences = [s.strip() for s in re.split(r'\.(?=\s|$)', text) if s.strip()]
    vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
    doc_term_matrix = vectorizer.fit_transform(sentences)
    
    return (pd.DataFrame(doc_term_matrix.toarray(), columns=vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()),)

def matrix_sparsity(output, ground_truth):
    output_df = output[0]
    ground_truth_df = ground_truth[0]
    total_elements = output_df.size
    non_zero_elements = output_df.astype(bool).sum().sum()
    sparsity_output = 1 - (non_zero_elements / total_elements) if total_elements > 0 else 0
    total_elements = ground_truth_df.size
    non_zero_elements = ground_truth_df.astype(bool).sum().sum()
    sparsity_ground_truth = 1 - (non_zero_elements / total_elements) if total_elements > 0 else 0
    result = abs(sparsity_output - sparsity_ground_truth)
    return 1 - result

def task_func(csv_file_path: str, title: str):
    # Load the CSV file into a DataFrame

    plt.show()

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime

# Constants
PLOT_TITLE = 'Square root plot'
X_LABEL = 'x'
Y_LABEL = 'sqrt(x)'
TIME_FORMAT = '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'

def task_func(result):
    # Extract values associated with the key 'from_user'
    x_values = [d['from_user'] for d in result if 'from_user' in d]
    
    # Calculate square root values and round to 2 decimals
    sqrt_values = np.round(np.sqrt(x_values), 2)
    
    # Plotting
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(x_values, sqrt_values, marker='o')
    ax.set_title(PLOT_TITLE)
    ax.set_xlabel(X_LABEL)
    ax.set_ylabel(Y_LABEL)
    
    # Annotate with current date and time
    current_time = datetime.now().strftime(TIME_FORMAT)
    ax.annotate(current_time, xy=(0.5, 0.95), xycoords='axes fraction', ha='center', fontsize=10, color='gray')
    
    plt.grid()
    plt.show()
    
    return sqrt_values, ax

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import ast
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(csv_file):
    # Load e-mail data from a CSV file
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_file)
    
    # Assume 'list' column contains string representations of lists
    df['list'] = df['list'].apply(ast.literal_eval)
    
    # Calculate sum, mean, and standard deviation for each list
    df['sum'] = df['list'].apply(np.sum)
    df['mean'] = df['list'].apply(np.mean)
    df['std'] = df['list'].apply(np.std)

    # Draw histogram of the mean values
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    hist_axes = sns.histplot(df['mean'], bins=10, kde=True)
    hist_axes.set_title('Histogram of Mean Values')
    hist_axes.set_xlabel('Mean Values')
    hist_axes.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    # Return DataFrame and histogram plot
    return (df, hist_axes)

import pandas as pd
import os
import numpy as np
import ast
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(directory):
    # Traverse the directory for CSV files
    csv_files = [f for f in os.listdir(directory) if f.endswith('.csv')]
    
    # Check if there are CSV files present
    if not csv_files:
        return pd.DataFrame(columns=['email', 'list', 'sum', 'mean', 'median']), None
    
    # Get the CSV file with the longest filename
    longest_file = max(csv_files, key=len)
    longest_file_path = os.path.join(directory, longest_file)
    
    # Load the email data from the longest CSV file
    email_data = pd.read_csv(longest_file_path)
    
    # Convert the 'list' column from string representation of list to actual lists
    email_data['list'] = email_data['list'].apply(ast.literal_eval)
    
    # Calculate sum, mean, and median of the list associated with each email
    email_data['sum'] = email_data['list'].apply(sum)
    email_data['mean'] = email_data['list'].apply(np.mean)
    email_data['median'] = email_data['list'].apply(np.median)
    
    # Create a histogram of the median
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.hist(email_data['median'], bins='auto')
    ax.set_title('Histogram of Median Values')
    ax.set_xlabel('Median')
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    # Output tuple of DataFrame and histogram
    return email_data, ax

from scipy.stats import linregress
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

def task_func(data, column1, column2):
    if column1 not in data.columns or column2 not in data.columns:
        raise ValueError(f"Specified columns '{column1}' or '{column2}' do not exist in the DataFrame.")
    
    x = data[column1]
    y = data[column2]
    
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = linregress(x, y)
    
    plt.scatter(x, y, label='Original Data')
    plt.plot(x, slope * x + intercept, color='red', label='Fitted Line')
    plt.xlabel(column1)
    plt.ylabel(column2)
    plt.title('Linear Regression')
    plt.legend()
    ax = plt.gca()
    plt.show()
    
    return (slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err, ax)

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
import numpy as np

def task_func(data, n_clusters=3):
    if not isinstance(data, pd.DataFrame):
        raise ValueError("Input 'data' must be a pd.DataFrame.")
    if not isinstance(n_clusters, int) or n_clusters <= 1:
        raise ValueError("Input 'n_clusters' must be an integer greater than 1.")

    # Perform K-means clustering
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=n_clusters, random_state=42)
    cluster_labels = kmeans.fit_predict(data)

    # Plotting
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    scatter_ax = plt.gca()
    scatter = scatter_ax.scatter(data.iloc[:, 0], data.iloc[:, 1], c=cluster_labels, cmap='viridis', label='Data Points')
    
    # Plot the centroids
    centroids = kmeans.cluster_centers_
    scatter_ax.scatter(centroids[:, 0], centroids[:, 1], c='red', marker='X', s=200, label='Centroids')

    # Add title and legend
    scatter_ax.set_title('K-means Clustering')
    scatter_ax.set_xlabel('Feature 1')
    scatter_ax.set_ylabel('Feature 2')
    plt.legend()
    plt.colorbar(scatter)

    return cluster_labels, scatter_ax

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from itertools import cycle

def task_func(df, groups=['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']):
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame):
        raise ValueError("Input 'df' must be a DataFrame.")
    
    required_columns = ['date', 'value']
    if not all(col in df.columns for col in required_columns):
        raise ValueError(f"Input 'df' must contain the required columns: {required_columns}.")

    df['ordinal_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date']).apply(lambda x: x.toordinal())
    
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    colors = cycle(plt.cm.tab10.colors)
    
    for group in groups:
        group_data = df[df['group'] == group]
        ax.scatter(group_data['ordinal_date'], group_data['value'], label=group, color=next(colors))
    
    ax.set_title('Scatterplot of Values for Each Group Over Time')
    ax.set_xlabel('Date (ordinal)')
    ax.set_ylabel('Value')
    ax.legend(title='Groups')
    
    return (ax,)

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def task_func(df):
    # Validate input DataFrame
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame):
        raise ValueError("Input is not a valid DataFrame.")
    if 'date' not in df.columns or 'value' not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError("DataFrame must contain 'date' and 'value' columns.")
    if not pd.api.types.is_datetime64_any_dtype(df['date']):
        raise ValueError("The 'date' column must be in datetime format.")
    
    # Prepare data for regression
    df['date_ordinal'] = df['date'].apply(lambda x: x.toordinal())
    X = df[['date_ordinal']]
    y = df['value']
    
    # Create and fit the model
    model = LinearRegression()
    model.fit(X, y)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions = model.predict(X)
    
    # Plot the results
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.scatter(df['date_ordinal'], y, color='blue', label='Original values')
    ax.plot(df['date_ordinal'], predictions, color='red', label='Predicted values')
    ax.set_title('Value vs Date (Linear Regression Prediction)')
    ax.set_xlabel('Date (ordinal)')
    ax.set_ylabel('Value')
    ax.legend()
    
    # Return the model, predictions, and axes
    return model, predictions, ax

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df):
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame):
        raise ValueError("Input must be a pandas DataFrame.")
    if 'Date' not in df.columns or 'Sales' not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError("DataFrame must contain 'Date' and 'Sales' columns.")
    if df.empty:
        raise ValueError("DataFrame cannot be empty.")
    
    # Ensure 'Date' column is in datetime format
    df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])
    
    # Group by date and sum sales
    daily_turnover = df.groupby('Date')['Sales'].sum().reset_index()
    
    # Create the line chart
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(daily_turnover['Date'], daily_turnover['Sales'], marker='o')
    ax.set_title('Daily Turnover')
    ax.set_xlabel('Date')
    ax.set_ylabel('Sales')
    ax.grid()

    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    return (ax,)

import binascii
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

def task_func(hex_str):
    # Normalize hex string, remove '0x' or '\x' prefix if present
    hex_str = hex_str.replace('0x', '').replace('\\x', '')
    
    # Validate if the string is a valid hex
    if not all(c in '0123456789abcdefABCDEF' for c in hex_str):
        raise ValueError("Invalid hex string")

    # Convert hex string to bytes
    byte_data = binascii.unhexlify(hex_str)

    # Record the frequency of each byte
    byte_values, counts = np.unique(np.frombuffer(byte_data, dtype=np.uint8), return_counts=True)
    
    # Create DataFrame for byte frequencies
    byte_freq_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'Byte Value': byte_values,
        'Frequency': counts
    })

    # Plot the frequencies
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(byte_freq_df['Byte Value'], byte_freq_df['Frequency'])
    ax.set_xlabel('Byte Value')
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    ax.set_title('Byte Frequency Distribution')
    
    return byte_freq_df, ax

from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

def task_func(df):
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame) or df.empty:
        raise ValueError("Input must be a non-empty pandas DataFrame.")
    
    # Normalize the last column
    scaler = MinMaxScaler()
    last_column_name = df.columns[-1]
    normalized_values = scaler.fit_transform(df[[last_column_name]])
    normalized_df = df.copy()
    normalized_df[last_column_name] = normalized_values
    
    # Plot the normalized last column
    fig, plot_axes = plt.subplots()
    plot_axes.plot(normalized_df.index, normalized_df[last_column_name])
    plot_axes.set_title(f'Normalized Data of {last_column_name}')
    plot_axes.set_xlabel('Index')
    plot_axes.set_ylabel('Normalized Value')
    
    plt.show()  # Show the plot
    return normalized_df, plot_axes

from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df):
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame):
        raise ValueError("Input must be a DataFrame.")
    if df.empty:
        raise ValueError("DataFrame is empty.")
    
    pca = PCA(n_components=2)

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df):
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame) or df.empty:
        raise ValueError("Input must be a non-empty DataFrame.")
    
    numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns
    if len(numeric_cols) == 0:
        raise ValueError("No numeric columns found in the DataFrame.")
    
    hist_axes = []
    num_histograms = 0
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
    for col in numeric_cols:
        ax = plt.subplot(len(numeric_cols), 1, num_histograms + 1)
        df[col].hist(ax=ax, bins=10, edgecolor='black')
        ax.set_title(col)
        ax.set_xlabel("Value")
        ax.set_ylabel("Frequency")
        hist_axes.append(ax)
        num_histograms += 1
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
    
    return (hist_axes, num_histograms)

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(data_dict, data_keys):
    # Check if any keys in data_keys are found in data_dict
    if not any(key in data_dict for key in data_keys):
        raise ValueError("No keys in data_keys are found in data_dict")

    # Prepare the data for normalization
    data_to_normalize = pd.DataFrame({key: data_dict[key] for key in data_keys if key in data_dict})

    # Normalize the data using MinMaxScaler
    scaler = MinMaxScaler()
    normalized_data = scaler.fit_transform(data_to_normalize)

    # Create a DataFrame for normalized values
    normalized_df = pd.DataFrame(normalized_data, columns=data_to_normalize.columns)

    # Plotting the normalized data
    plot_axes = normalized_df.plot(figsize=(10, 6), title='Normalized Data')
    plot_axes.set_ylabel('Normalized Value')
    plt.legend(title='Data Keys')

    # Show the plot
    plt.show()

    return normalized_df, plot_axes

def task_func(text, rwidth=0.8):

        ax.set_title('No words found')

import re
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

def task_func(df):
    # Check if DataFrame is empty or lacks the required columns
    required_columns = ['Title', 'Views', 'Likes']
    if df.empty or not all(col in df.columns for col in required_columns):
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.set_visible(False)
        return (ax,)

    # Filter titles containing "how" or "what"
    filtered_df = df[df['Title'].str.contains(r'\b(how|what)\b', case=False, regex=True)]

    # Check if any titles match the criteria
    if filtered_df.empty:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.set_visible(False)
        return (ax,)
    
    # Calculate like ratios
    filtered_df['Like Ratio'] = filtered_df['Likes'] / filtered_df['Views']
    
    # Plotting
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(filtered_df['Title'], filtered_df['Like Ratio'])
    ax.set_xlabel('Video Title')
    ax.set_ylabel('Like Ratio')
    ax.set_title('Like Ratios of Videos with "How" or "What" in Title')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
    
    return (ax,)

import re
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

def task_func(df):
    if 'Title' not in df.columns or 'Content' not in df.columns:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.set_title('Empty Plot')
        ax.set_xticks([])
        ax.set_yticks([])
        return ax, True

    filtered_df = df[df['Title'].str.contains(r'\b(how|what)\b', case=False, na=False)]
    if filtered_df.empty:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.set_title('Empty Plot')
        ax.set_xticks([])
        ax.set_yticks([])
        return ax, True
    
    vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer()
    tfidf_matrix = vectorizer.fit_transform(filtered_df['Content'])
    feature_names = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

    sum_tfidf = tfidf_matrix.sum(axis=0).A1
    tfidf_scores = dict(zip(feature_names, sum_tfidf))
    
    sorted_items = sorted(tfidf_scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    labels, scores = zip(*sorted_items)

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(labels, scores)
    ax.set_ylabel('TF-IDF Score')
    ax.set_xticklabels(labels, rotation=90)
    plt.tight_layout()

    return ax, False

import heapq
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(l1, l2, N=10):
    # Calculate the absolute differences
    differences = [abs(a - b) for a, b in zip(l1, l2)]
    
    # Get the N biggest differences
    biggest_differences = heapq.nlargest(N, differences)
    
    # Square the differences and take the square root
    squared_differences = [math.sqrt(d ** 2) for d in biggest_differences]
    
    # Plot the squared differences
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(squared_differences, marker='o')
    ax.set_title(f'Top {N} Biggest Differences')
    ax.set_xlabel('Difference Index')
    ax.set_ylabel('Squared Differences')
    
    return (ax, squared_differences)

def task_func(data, value):

import numpy as np
import itertools
import json

def task_func(data_list, json_file_name="mean_values.json"):
    # Transpose the data list to group numeric values by their positions
    transposed_data = list(itertools.zip_longest(*data_list, fillvalue=0))
    
    # Calculate the mean for each position
    mean_values = {}
    for i, position_values in enumerate(transposed_data):
        numeric_values = [value for value in position_values if isinstance(value, (int, float))]
        if numeric_values:  # Only calculate mean if there are numeric values
            mean_values[f'Position {i}'] = np.mean(numeric_values)
    
    # Attempt to export results to a JSON file
    file_exported = False
    try:
        with open(json_file_name, 'w') as json_file:
            json.dump(mean_values, json_file)
        file_exported = True
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"An error occurred while exporting to JSON: {e}")
        
    return mean_values, file_exported

import itertools
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(elements, subset_size):
    # Generate all subsets of the given size
    subsets = list(itertools.combinations(elements, subset_size))
    
    # Calculate sums of the subsets
    sums = [sum(subset) for subset in subsets]
    
    # Draw a histogram of the sums
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.hist(sums, bins='auto', alpha=0.7, color='blue')
    ax.set_title('Histogram of Subset Sums')
    ax.set_xlabel('Sum')
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    return ax, subsets, sums

import pandas as pd
from scipy.stats import zscore
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df):
    if 'Date' not in df.columns or 'Value' not in df.columns:
        raise KeyError("DataFrame must contain 'Date' and 'Value' columns")
    
    # Convert 'Date' to datetime
    df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])
    
    # Split 'Value' lists into separate columns
    value_expanded = df['Value'].apply(pd.Series)
    
    # Calculate Z-scores
    zscore_df = value_expanded.apply(zscore, axis=1)
    
    # Add 'Date' as the first column
    zscore_df.insert(0, 'Date', df['Date'])
    
    # Create the box plot of Z-scores over time
    boxplot_fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    plt.boxplot(zscore_df.iloc[:, 1:], labels=zscore_df.columns[1:])
    plt.title("Z-Scores Over Time")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Z-Score")
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    return zscore_df, boxplot_fig

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df):
    if df.empty:
        return (0, 0)
    
    # Assuming the date column is the first column and numeric lists are in subsequent columns
    numeric_data = pd.DataFrame(df.iloc[:, 1].tolist(), index=df.index)
    
    # Performing PCA
    pca = PCA()
    pca.fit(numeric_data)
    explained_variance_ratio = pca.explained_variance_ratio_
    
    # Plotting the explained variance ratio
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(range(1, len(explained_variance_ratio) + 1), explained_variance_ratio)
    ax.set_title("Explained Variance Ratio of Principal Components")
    ax.set_xlabel("Principal Component")
    ax.set_ylabel("Explained Variance Ratio")
    
    plt.show()
    
    return (explained_variance_ratio, ax)

import numpy as np
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
import re

def task_func(example_str):
    # Extract text not enclosed in square brackets
    extracted_text = re.sub(r'\[.*?\]', '', example_str).strip()
    
    # If no valid text, return empty results
    if not extracted_text:
        return (extracted_text, {})
    
    # Create TF-IDF vectorizer
    vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer()
    tfidf_matrix = vectorizer.fit_transform([extracted_text])
    
    # Get feature names and their corresponding TF-IDF scores
    feature_names = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()
    tfidf_scores = tfidf_matrix.toarray()[0]
    
    # Create a dictionary of words and their TF-IDF scores
    tfidf_dict = {feature_names[i]: tfidf_scores[i] for i in range(len(feature_names))}
    
    return (extracted_text, tfidf_dict)

import re
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from nltk.probability import FreqDist

def task_func(example_str, top_n=30):
    # Remove text inside square brackets
    cleaned_str = re.sub(r'\[.*?\]', '', example_str)
    
    # Split the cleaned string into words
    words = cleaned_str.split()
    
    # Calculate frequency distribution
    freq_dist = FreqDist(words)
    
    # Plot the frequency distribution
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    freq_plot = plt.axes()
    freq_dist.plot(30, cumulative=False)
    plt.title('Word Frequency Distribution')
    plt.xlabel('Words')
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')
    
    # Get the most common words
    common_words = dict(freq_dist.most_common(top_n))
    
    return freq_plot, common_words

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Constants
COLORS = ['r', 'g', 'b']

def task_func(df, group_col, value_col):
    # Check for non-numeric values in the specified value column
    if not pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df[value_col]):
        raise TypeError("The 'Value' column has non-numeric values.")
    
    # Group and summarize data
    grouped_data = df.groupby(group_col)[value_col].agg(['mean', 'std', 'count']).reset_index()
    
    # Number of groups
    num_groups = len(grouped_data)
    
    # Create bar positions
    indices = np.arange(num_groups)
    bar_width = 0.4
    
    # Create bar chart
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    
    # Create bars with error bars
    for i in range(num_groups):
        color = COLORS[i % len(COLORS)]
        ax.bar(indices[i], grouped_data['mean'][i], yerr=grouped_data['std'][i], 
               width=bar_width, color=color, capsize=5, label=grouped_data[group_col][i])
    
    # Set labels and title
    ax.set_xlabel(group_col)
    ax.set_ylabel(value_col)
    title = f"Bar chart of {value_col} by {group_col}"
    ax.set_title(title)
    
    # Set x-ticks
    ax.set_xticks(indices)
    ax.set_xticklabels(grouped_data[group_col])

    # Show legend
    ax.legend()
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    return ax, title

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Constants for pie chart colors
COLORS = ['r', 'g', 'b', 'y', 'm']

def task_func(df, col, title=None):
    # Validate input
    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame):
        raise ValueError("The input df must be DataFrame.")
    if df.empty:
        raise ValueError("The DataFrame must not be empty.")
    if col not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError(f"The specified column '{col}' must be present in the DataFrame.")

    # Count unique values
    unique_counts = df[col].value_counts().to_dict()

    # Plot pie chart
    pie_axes = plt.figure().add_subplot(1, 1, 1)
    pie_axes.pie(unique_counts.values(), labels=unique_counts.keys(), colors=COLORS[:len(unique_counts)], autopct='%1.1f%%')
    if title:
        pie_axes.set_title(title)

    plt.show()
    
    return pie_axes, unique_counts

import pandas as pd
import logging

# Set up basic configuration for logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')

def task_func(sheet_name, excel_file_location="test.xlsx", csv_file_location="test.csv"):
    try:
        # Read the Excel file
        df = pd.read_excel(excel_file_location, sheet_name=sheet_name)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        logging.error("The specified Excel file was not found.")
        raise
    except ValueError:
        logging.error("The specified sheet name does not exist in the Excel file.")
        raise
    
    # Check if the DataFrame contains only numerical data
    if not df.applymap(lambda x: isinstance(x, (int, float))).all().all():
        logging.error("The Excel file contains non-numerical data.")
        raise ValueError("The Excel file contains non-numerical data.")
    
    # Convert the DataFrame to a CSV file
    df.to_csv(csv_file_location, index=False)
    logging.info(f"CSV file created at {csv_file_location}")

    # Calculate the sum of each column
    column_sums = df.sum().to_dict()
    
    return column_sums, csv_file_location

from datetime import datetime
from collections import defaultdict
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(activities):
    if not all(isinstance(activity, datetime) for activity in activities):
        raise TypeError("All activities must be datetime objects.")
    
    activities_count = defaultdict(int)
    
    for activity in activities:
        day_of_week = activity.strftime('%A')
        activities_count[day_of_week] += 1

    days = ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday', 'Sunday']
    counts = [activities_count[day] for day in days]

    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(days, counts)
    ax.set_xlabel('Day of the Week')
    ax.set_ylabel('Number of Activities')
    ax.set_title('Weekly Activity')

    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    return (ax,)

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import os
import numpy as np

def task_func(file_path='arena.csv', target_column='Index', seed=42):
    if not os.path.isfile(file_path):
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"The file at {file_path} does not exist.")
    
    df = pd.read_csv(file_path)
    
    if target_column not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError(f"The target column '{target_column}' is not found in the CSV file.")
    
    df = df.dropna()
    
    if df.isin([float('inf'), -float('inf')]).any().any():
        raise ValueError("The input data contains infinity values.")
    
    if (df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']) > np.finfo(np.float32).max).any().any():
        raise ValueError("A value too large for dtype('float32') is present in the input data.")
    
    X = df.drop(columns=[target_column])
    y = df[target_column]
    
    model = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=seed)
    model.fit(X, y)
    
    importances = model.feature_importances_
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    sns.barplot(x=importances, y=X.columns)
    plt.title('Feature Importances')
    plt.xlabel('Importance')
    plt.ylabel('Features')
    plt.show()
    
    ax = plt.gca()
    
    return ax, importances

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

def task_func(mu, sigma, num_samples=1000, seed=77):
    np.random.seed(seed)
    samples = np.random.normal(mu, sigma, num_samples)
    
    fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6))
    
    # Histogram
    axs[0].hist(samples, bins=30, density=True, alpha=0.6, color='g')
    xmin, xmax = axs[0].get_xlim()
    x = np.linspace(xmin, xmax, 100)
    p = stats.norm.pdf(x, mu, sigma)
    axs[0].plot(x, p, 'k', linewidth=2)
    axs[0].set_title('Histogram of Samples')
    axs[0].set_xlabel('Value')
    axs[0].set_ylabel('Density')

    # Q-Q plot
    stats.probplot(samples, dist="norm", plot=axs[1])
    axs[1].set_title('Q-Q Plot')

    plt.tight_layout()
    return (fig,)

def qq_plot_correlation(output, ground_truth):
    import numpy as np
    # 提取 output 中的 matplotlib Figure 对象
    fig_output = output[0]
    ax_qq_output = fig_output.get_axes()[1]

    # 从 Q-Q 图的 Line2D 对象中提取数据
    if not ax_qq_output.lines:
        return None  # 如果没有数据，直接返回 None

    qq_line = ax_qq_output.lines[0]  # 获取 Q-Q 图的线
    x_qq_output, y_qq_output = qq_line.get_data()  # 提取 (x, y) 数据

    if len(x_qq_output) == 0 or len(y_qq_output) == 0:
        return None  # 如果数据为空，返回 None

    # 计算 output 的 Q-Q 图相关系数
    correlation_coefficient = np.corrcoef(x_qq_output, y_qq_output)[0, 1]

    # 提取 ground truth 中的 Q-Q 图数据
    fig_ground_truth = ground_truth[0]
    ax_qq_ground_truth = fig_ground_truth.get_axes()[1]
    if not ax_qq_ground_truth.lines:
        return None  # 如果没有数据，直接返回 None

    qq_line_ground_truth = ax_qq_ground_truth.lines[0]
    x_qq_ground_truth, y_qq_ground_truth = qq_line_ground_truth.get_data()

    if len(x_qq_ground_truth) == 0 or len(y_qq_ground_truth) == 0:
        return None  # 如果数据为空，返回 None

    # 计算 ground truth 的 Q-Q 图相关系数
    ground_truth_correlation = np.corrcoef(x_qq_ground_truth, y_qq_ground_truth)[0, 1]

    # 返回两者相关系数的绝对差值
    difference = np.abs(correlation_coefficient - ground_truth_correlation)

    # 将差值转换为 [0,1] 范围内的数值，1 表示最好的结果
    score = 1 - difference
    score = np.clip(score, 0, 1)  # 确保结果在 [0,1] 范围内

    return score

from datetime import datetime

def task_func(excel_directory: str, file_name: str, column_name: str, start_date: str, end_date: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    # Check if the file exists
    file_path = os.path.join(excel_directory, file_name)
    if not os.path.isfile(file_path):
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"The file {file_name} does not exist in the directory {excel_directory}.")

    # Load the Excel file into a DataFrame
    df = pd.read_excel(file_path)

    # Validate the date format
    try:
        start_date = datetime.strptime(start_date, "%Y-%m-%d")
        end_date = datetime.strptime(end_date, "%Y-%m-%d")
    except ValueError:
        raise ValueError("start_date and end_date must be in the format 'YYYY-MM-DD'.")

    # Check if the specified column exists in the DataFrame
    if column_name not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError(f"The column '{column_name}' does not exist in the DataFrame.")

    # Convert the column to datetime
    df[column_name] = pd.to_datetime(df[column_name], errors='coerce')

    # Filter the DataFrame based on date range
    filtered_data = df[(df[column_name] >= start_date) & (df[column_name] <= end_date)]

    return (filtered_data,)

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(data, column='c'):
    # Convert the input dictionary to a DataFrame
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)
    
    # Remove the specified column
    if column in df.columns:
        df = df.drop(columns=[column])
    
    # Select only the numeric columns
    numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include=['number'])
    
    # Check if there's any numeric data to work with
    if numeric_df.empty:
        return (None,)
    
    # Compute the correlation matrix
    correlation_matrix = numeric_df.corr()
    
    # Create a heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
    heatmap_axes = sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap='coolwarm', square=True)
    
    # Show the heatmap
    plt.show()
    
    return (heatmap_axes,)

def columns_removed(output, ground_truth):
    output_data = output[0]  # 提取 Axes 对象
    ground_truth_data = ground_truth[0]
    
    # 假设 ground_truth 包含初始的列数
    initial_columns = len(ground_truth_data.columns)
    remaining_columns = len(output_data.collections[0].get_array()) if output_data.collections else 0  # 假设只有存在数值列时才会创建热图
    
    # 计算保留的列数占初始列数的比例
    retention_ratio = remaining_columns / initial_columns if initial_columns > 0 else 0
    
    # 返回一个在 [0,1] 范围内的数值，1 表示最好的结果（即没有移除任何列）
    return retention_ratio

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dense
from keras.optimizers import SGD

def task_func(X, Y, learning_rate=0.01, epochs=100):
    # Split the data into a training set (75%) and a test set (25%)
    X_train, X_test, Y_train, Y_test = train_test_split(X, Y, test_size=0.25, random_state=42)

    # Construct the Sequential model
    model = Sequential()
    model.add(Dense(1, input_dim=2, activation='sigmoid'))

    # Compile the model
    model.compile(loss='binary_crossentropy', optimizer=SGD(learning_rate=learning_rate), metrics=['accuracy'])

    # Fit the model to the training data
    history = model.fit(X_train, Y_train, validation_data=(X_test, Y_test), epochs=epochs, verbose=0)

    # Plotting the training and validation loss
    ax = plt.gca()
    ax.plot(history.history['loss'], label='Train')
    ax.plot(history.history['val_loss'], label='Test')
    ax.set_title('Model loss')
    ax.set_xlabel('Epoch')
    ax.set_ylabel('Loss')
    ax.legend()

    # Return the trained model and the Axes object
    return model, ax

def task_func(X, Y):
    # Divide the input data into training and test sets (70% training, 30% test)
    X_train, X_test, Y_train, Y_test = train_test_split(X, Y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

    # Create a Keras Sequential model with one hidden layer using a sigmoid activation function
    model = keras.Sequential([
        keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='sigmoid', input_shape=(X_train.shape[1],)),
        keras.layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
    ])

def task_func(X, Y):

    # Fit the model to the training data in a non-verbose mode

    # Predict probabilities for the test set
    y_scores = model.predict(X_test).ravel()

    precision, recall, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_scores)

    # Plot the Precision-Recall curve
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(recall, precision, marker='.')
    ax.set_xlabel('Recall')
    ax.set_ylabel('Precision')
    ax.set_title('Precision-Recall Curve')

    # Return the trained model and the axes object
    return model, ax

def pr_curve_quality(output, ground_truth):
    import numpy as np
    lines_output = output[1].get_lines()
    if not lines_output:
        return 0  # If no lines, return the worst case scenario

    precision_output, recall_output = lines_output[0].get_data()

    # Extract precision and recall data from the ground_truth
    lines_gt = ground_truth[1].get_lines()
    if not lines_gt:
        return 0  # If no lines, return the worst case scenario

    precision_gt, recall_gt = lines_gt[0].get_data()

    # Ensure both precision-recall curves have the same recall points for comparison
    combined_recall = np.union1d(recall_output, recall_gt)
    precision_output_interpolated = np.interp(combined_recall, recall_output, precision_output)
    precision_gt_interpolated = np.interp(combined_recall, recall_gt, precision_gt)

    # Compute the area between the precision-recall curves (for simplicity, use absolute difference)
    area_between_curves = np.trapz(np.abs(precision_output_interpolated - precision_gt_interpolated), combined_recall)

    # Compute the maximum possible area (area under a perfect precision-recall curve)
    max_possible_area = np.trapz(np.ones_like(combined_recall), combined_recall)

    # Convert the area between curves to a value between 0 and 1
    quality_score = 1 - (area_between_curves / max_possible_area)
    
    # Ensure the quality score is within the range [0, 1]
    quality_score = max(0, min(1, quality_score))
    
    return quality_score

from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def task_func(df1, df2, column1="feature1", column2="feature2"):
    # Merging datasets
    merged_df = pd.merge(df1, df2, left_on='id', right_on='id')
    
    # Extracting the features for clustering
    features = merged_df[[column1, column2]].values
    
    # Performing KMeans clustering
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=2, n_init=10, random_state=42)
    labels = kmeans.fit_predict(features)
    
    # Creating the scatterplot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    scatter = ax.scatter(merged_df[column1], merged_df[column2], c=labels, cmap='viridis')
    ax.set_xlabel(column1)
    ax.set_ylabel(column2)
    ax.set_title('KMeans Clustering')
    plt.colorbar(scatter, ax=ax, label='Cluster')
    
    return labels.astype(np.int32), ax

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency

def task_func(df1, df2, column1="feature1", column2="feature2"):
    # Merge the two dataframes on the 'id' column
    merged_df = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id')

    # Create a contingency table
    contingency_table = pd.crosstab(merged_df[column1], merged_df[column2])

    # Perform the Chi-Squared independence test
    chi2, p, _, _ = chi2_contingency(contingency_table)

    # Create a heatmap of the contingency table
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    heatmap = sns.heatmap(contingency_table, annot=True, fmt='g', cmap='Blues')
    plt.title('Contingency Table Heatmap')
    plt.xlabel(column2)
    plt.ylabel(column1)
    plt.show()

    return (p, heatmap)

import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(
    P: np.ndarray,
    T: np.ndarray,
    n_clusters: int = 3,
    random_state: int = 0,
    n_init: int = 10,
) -> (np.ndarray, plt.Axes):
    # Calculate the product of matrix P and tensor T
    result = np.matmul(P, T)  # Assuming the last dimension of T aligns with the number of columns in P
    
    # Flatten the result
    flattened_result = result.reshape(-1, result.shape[-1])

    # Apply KMeans clustering
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=n_clusters, random_state=random_state, n_init=n_init)
    kmeans.fit(flattened_result)
    cluster_result = kmeans.labels_

    # Visualization of the clustering
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.scatter(flattened_result[:, 0], flattened_result[:, 1], c=cluster_result, cmap='viridis')
    ax.set_title('KMeans Clustering Visualization')
    
    return cluster_result, ax

def mse_cluster_centers(output, ground_truth):
    from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
    output_centers = output[0]
    ground_truth_centers = ground_truth[0]
    
    # Calculate the Mean Squared Error
    mse_value = mean_squared_error(ground_truth_centers, output_centers)
    
    # Assuming the maximum possible MSE is the MSE when the centers are as far apart as possible
    # For example, if the centers are in a unit sphere, the maximum MSE would be the square of the sphere's diameter
    max_possible_mse = np.linalg.norm(np.ptp(ground_truth_centers, axis=0))**2
    
    # Normalize the MSE to be in the range [0, 1], where 0 is the best
    normalized_mse = 1 - (mse_value / max_possible_mse)
    
    # Ensure the normalized MSE is within the [0, 1] range
    normalized_mse = max(0, min(1, normalized_mse))
    
    return normalized_mse

from scipy.spatial.distance import cdist
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(n_samples=200, centers=4, plot_path=None, random_seed=None):
    # Generate synthetic dataset
    X, y = make_blobs(n_samples=n_samples, centers=centers, random_state=random_seed)
    
    # Calculate pairwise Euclidean distances
    distances = cdist(X, X, metric='euclidean')

    # Create a scatter plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    scatter = plt.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], c=y, cmap='viridis', marker='o')
    plt.title('Synthetic 2D Dataset')
    plt.xlabel('Feature 1')
    plt.ylabel('Feature 2')
    plt.colorbar(scatter, label='Cluster Label')

    # Save or show the plot
    if plot_path is None:
        ax = plt.gca()
        plt.grid(True)
        plt.show()
        return distances, ax
    else:
        plt.savefig(plot_path)
        plt.close()
        return distances, None

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

def task_func(myList, n_clusters):
    if not isinstance(myList, list) or not all(isinstance(point, (list, tuple)) and len(point) == 2 for point in myList):
        raise ValueError("Input must be a list of 2D points.")
    if not isinstance(n_clusters, int) or n_clusters <= 0:
        raise ValueError("Number of clusters must be a positive integer.")
    
    data = np.array(myList)
    
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=n_clusters, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    kmeans.fit(data)
    centroids = kmeans.cluster_centers_
    
    plt.figure()
    plt.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1], c=kmeans.labels_, cmap='viridis')
    plt.scatter(centroids[:, 0], centroids[:, 1], color='red', marker='x', s=100, label='Centroids')
    plt.legend()
    ax = plt.gca()
    
    return ax, centroids

def centroid_distance(output, ground_truth):
    import numpy as np
    output_centroids = output[1]  # extract centroids from output tuple
    ground_truth_centroids = ground_truth[1]
    
    # Calculate the average Euclidean distance between the centroids
    avg_distance = np.mean(np.linalg.norm(output_centroids - ground_truth_centroids, axis=1))
    
    # Assuming the maximum possible distance is the distance between the furthest points in the ground truth centroids
    max_possible_distance = np.max(np.linalg.norm(ground_truth_centroids - np.mean(ground_truth_centroids, axis=0), axis=1))
    
    # Normalize the average distance to be in the range [0, 1], where 0 is the best
    normalized_distance = avg_distance / max_possible_distance
    
    # Since we want 1 to be the best, we need to invert the normalized distance
    quality_score = 1 - normalized_distance
    
    # Ensure the quality score is within the [0, 1] range
    quality_score = max(0, min(1, quality_score))
    
    return quality_score

def task_func(data):
    # Initialize total sales dictionary
    total_sales = collections.defaultdict(int)

    # Iterate through the list of dictionaries
    for entry in data:

import json
import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(input_file):
    with open(input_file, 'r') as file:
        data = json.load(file)
    
    stats = defaultdict(list)
    
    # Collecting all values for each key
    for entry in data:
        for key, value in entry.items():
            stats[key].append(value)
    
    result = {}
    plots = []

    # Calculating mean and median
    for key, values in stats.items():
        mean_value = np.mean(values)
        median_value = np.median(values)
        result[key] = {'mean': mean_value, 'median': median_value}
        
        # Visualizing with bar charts
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.bar(['Mean', 'Median'], [mean_value, median_value], color=['blue', 'orange'])
        ax.set_title(f'Statistics for {key}')
        ax.set_ylabel('Value')
        plots.append(ax)

    return (result, plots)

import json
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict

def task_func(input_file: str) -> tuple:
    # Read JSON data from file
    with open(input_file, 'r') as file:
        data = json.load(file)

    # Calculate mean and median for each key
    results = defaultdict(dict)
    for key in data[0].keys():
        values = [d[key] for d in data if key in d]
        if values:  # Ensure there are values to calculate
            results[key]['mean'] = np.mean(values)
            results[key]['median'] = np.median(values)

    # Convert to DataFrame for visualization
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)
    df_melted = df.melt(var_name='X_', value_name='Y_')

    # Create box plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    ax = sns.boxplot(x='X_', y='Y_', data=df_melted)
    ax.set_title('Box Plot of Values for Each Key')
    ax.set_xlabel('Keys')
    ax.set_ylabel('Values')

    return results, ax

def missing_values_percentage(output, ground_truth):
    output = output[0]  # Extract the first element of the output
    ground_truth = ground_truth[0]  # Extract the first element of the ground truth
    
    total_keys = len(ground_truth)
    missing_score_sum = 0
    
    for key in ground_truth.keys():
        # Calculate percentage of missing values for each key
        if key not in output:
            missing_score_sum += 1  # If key not found, add 1 to the sum
        else:
            ground_truth_values = ground_truth[key]
            if len(ground_truth_values) == 0:
                continue  # Skip if no values present in ground truth
            else:
                output_values = output.get(key, {}).values()  # Get values for the key from output
                num_missing = len(ground_truth_values) - len(output_values)
                missing_percentage = (num_missing / len(ground_truth_values))
                missing_score_sum += missing_percentage
    
    # Calculate the average missing score across all keys
    average_missing_score = missing_score_sum / total_keys
    
    # Invert the score so that 1 is the best case (no missing values)
    # and 0 is the worst case (all values missing)
    score = 1 - average_missing_score
    
    # Ensure the score is within [0, 1] range
    score = max(0, min(score, 1))
    
    return score

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(df: pd.DataFrame) -> (Counter, plt.Axes):
    if df.empty:
        raise ValueError("The DataFrame is empty.")
    
    # Round down the age and check for negative values
    df['age'] = np.floor(df['age']).astype(int)
    if (df['age'] < 0).any():
        raise ValueError("Age must not be negative.")
    
    # Identify duplicates
    duplicates = df[df.duplicated(subset='name', keep=False)]
    if duplicates.empty:
        return Counter(), None
    
    # Calculate age distribution for duplicate names
    age_distribution = Counter(duplicates['age'])
    
    # Create histogram plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    bins = np.arange(duplicates['age'].min() - 0.5, duplicates['age'].max() + 1.5, 1)
    sns.histplot(duplicates['age'], bins=bins, kde=False)
    plt.xlabel('Age')
    plt.ylabel('Count')
    plt.title('Age Distribution for Duplicate Names')
    
    # Retrieve the plot Axes
    histogram_plot = plt.gca()
    plt.show()
    
    return age_distribution, histogram_plot

import numpy as np
from collections import Counter
from scipy.stats import norm
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

def task_func(df, bins=4):
    # Count duplicates
    duplicates_count = Counter(df['value'])
    
    # Filter to keep only duplicates
    duplicates_count = {k: v for k, v in duplicates_count.items() if v > 1}
    
    # Plotting the histogram
    histogram_axes = plt.axes()
    counts, bins, patches = histogram_axes.hist(df['value'], bins=bins, color='green', alpha=0.6, edgecolor='black')
    
    # Fit a normal distribution to the data
    mu, std = norm.fit(df['value'])
    
    # Plot the normal distribution curve
    xmin, xmax = histogram_axes.get_xlim()
    x = np.linspace(xmin, xmax, 100)
    p = norm.pdf(x, mu, std)
    
    histogram_axes.plot(x, p * len(df) * (bins[1] - bins[0]), 'k', linewidth=2)
    
    # Setting titles and labels
    histogram_axes.set_title("Distribution")
    histogram_axes.set_xlabel("Value")
    histogram_axes.set_ylabel("Frequency")
    
    plt.show()
    
    return (Counter(duplicates_count), histogram_axes)

def normal_distribution_fit_check(output, ground_truth):
    import numpy as np
    # Unpack the output and ground truth
    output_counter, output_axes = output
    gt_counter, gt_axes = ground_truth
    
    # Check if the Counter objects are the same
    counters_equal = output_counter == gt_counter
    
    # Check if the histograms are similar by comparing the bins and frequencies
    output_hist_data = output_axes.patches
    gt_hist_data = gt_axes.patches
    
    if len(output_hist_data) != len(gt_hist_data):
        return False  # The number of bins doesn't match, so they are not similar
    
    # Compare each bin's height (frequency)
    for o_patch, gt_patch in zip(output_hist_data, gt_hist_data):
        if not np.isclose(o_patch.get_height(), gt_patch.get_height(), rtol=1e-2):
            return False  # Bin heights differ too much
    
    # If both the counters and histograms are close, return True
    return counters_equal

from collections import Counter  
import numpy as np  
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  
import itertools  

def task_func(list_of_menuitems, title="Menu Distribution", color="blue", width=1.0):  
    # Flatten the nested list of menu items
    flattened_items = itertools.chain.from_iterable(list_of_menuitems)
    
    # Count occurrences of each item
    item_counts = Counter(flattened_items)
    
    # Sort items alphabetically
    sorted_items = sorted(item_counts.items())
    
    # Separate the items and their counts for plotting
    items, counts = zip(*sorted_items)
    
    # Create the histogram plot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(items, counts, color=color, width=width)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel('Menu Items')
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    
    # Return the Axes object and the item counts
    return ax, dict(item_counts)

def task_func(a, b, items=ITEMS):

    combined_list = a + b

    freq_dict = collections.Counter(item for item in combined_list if item in items)

    ax.set_title('Frequency of Predefined Items')

import pandas as pd
from scipy.spatial import distance
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(a, b):
    # Calculate the Euclidean distance between the two lists
    euclidean_dist = distance.euclidean(a, b)
    
    # Create a DataFrame from the lists with indices 'A' and 'B'
    df = pd.DataFrame({'A': a, 'B': b})
    
    # Plot the values and display the Euclidean distance
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    df.plot(ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(f'Euclidean Distance: {euclidean_dist:.2f}')
    ax.legend(['A', 'B'])
    
    # Return the tuple with the required outputs
    return (euclidean_dist, df, ax)

import unicodedata
import csv
from collections import Counter
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def normalize_text(text):
    # Normalize the text to ASCII
    return unicodedata.normalize('NFKD', text).encode('ascii', 'ignore').decode('utf-8')

def task_func(csv_file):
    try:
        with open(csv_file, mode='r', encoding='utf-8') as file:
            reader = csv.reader(file)
            words = []
            for row in reader:
                for cell in row:
                    normalized = normalize_text(cell)
                    words.extend(normalized.split())
    except FileNotFoundError:
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"The file {csv_file} cannot be found.")
    except IOError as e:
        raise IOError(f"An error occurred reading the file {csv_file}: {e}")

    # Count the words
    word_counts = Counter(words)

    # Get the 10 most common words
    common_words = word_counts.most_common(10)

    # Prepare the data for plotting
    labels, values = zip(*common_words)

    # Create a bar plot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.bar(labels, values)
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    ax.set_title('10 Most Common Words')

    return ax, common_words

import numpy as np

def task_func(data_dict, letter):
    # Convert the input dictionary to a DataFrame
    df = pd.DataFrame(data_dict)
    
    # Filter rows where 'Word' column values start with the specified letter
    filtered_df = df[df['Word'].str.startswith(letter, na=False)]
    
    # Calculate the lengths of the filtered words
    word_lengths = filtered_df['Word'].str.len()
    
    # Calculate basic statistics
    mean_length = word_lengths.mean()
    median_length = word_lengths.median()
    mode_length = word_lengths.mode()[0] if not word_lengths.mode().empty else None
    
    # Create a statistics dictionary
    stats_dict = {
        'mean': mean_length,
        'median': median_length,
        'mode': mode_length
    }
    
    return (stats_dict, word_lengths)

    data = np.array(flat_list).reshape(-1, 1)

    # Convert standardized data back to a list

    ax.set_title('Standardized Values')
    ax.set_xlabel('Index')
    ax.set_ylabel('Standardized Value')

def standardization_mse(output, ground_truth):
    import numpy as np
    output_standardized = output[0]  # extract the standardized list from tuple
    ground_truth_standardized = ground_truth[0]
    
    mse = np.mean((np.array(ground_truth_standardized) - np.array(output_standardized))**2)
    
    # Assuming the data is standardized to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1,
    # the maximum possible MSE is 1 (when all predictions are the exact opposite of the true values).
    max_possible_mse = 1
    
    # Normalize the MSE to be within [0, 1], where 0 is the worst and 1 is the best
    normalized_mse = 1 - mse / max_possible_mse
    
    # Ensure the normalized score is within [0, 1] range
    normalized_mse = max(0, min(normalized_mse, 1))
    
    return normalized_mse

def normal_distribution_fit(output, ground_truth):
    from scipy.stats import norm
    from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
    
    # 获取 task_func 的输出，即 ax 和 扁平化后的列表
    ax, flattened_list_pred = output
    
    # 对扁平化后的列表拟合正态分布，获取均值和标准差
    mu_pred, std_pred = norm.fit(flattened_list_pred)
    
    # 获取真实数据的 ax 和 扁平化后的列表
    ax, flattened_list_true = ground_truth
    
    # 对真实数据的扁平化后的列表拟合正态分布，获取均值和标准差
    mu_true, std_true = norm.fit(flattened_list_true)
    
    mse_mu = mean_squared_error([mu_true], [mu_pred])
    mse_std = mean_squared_error([std_true], [std_pred])
    
    # 定义最大可能的MSE值，这里假设为1，这可能需要根据实际情况进行调整
    max_possible_mse = 1
    
    # 归一化MSE值到[0, 1]范围，其中1是最佳情况
    normalized_mse_mu = 1 - (mse_mu / max_possible_mse)
    normalized_mse_std = 1 - (mse_std / max_possible_mse)
    
    # 确保归一化后的值在[0, 1]范围内
    normalized_mse_mu = max(0, min(normalized_mse_mu, 1))
    normalized_mse_std = max(0, min(normalized_mse_std, 1))
    
    # 返回归一化后的MSE值，这里我们返回两个值，但可以根据需要返回一个综合评分
    return normalized_mse_mu, normalized_mse_std

from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Constants
N_COMPONENTS = 2

def task_func(L):
    # Convert list of lists to a 2D numpy array
    data = np.array(L)
    
    # Initialize PCA
    pca = PCA(n_components=N_COMPONENTS)
    
    # Fit and transform the data
    pca_result = pca.fit_transform(data)
    
    # Create scatter plot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.scatter(pca_result[:, 0], pca_result[:, 1])
    ax.set_title('PCA Result')
    ax.set_xlabel('Principal Component 1')

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    return hist_plot, year_distribution

def visual_quality(output, ground_truth):
    from skimage.metrics import structural_similarity as ssim
    
    output_image = output[0]  # This would require a method to convert the figure to an image array
    ground_truth_image = ground_truth[0]  # Assuming the ground truth is also converted to an image array
    
    # Calculate SSIM, which ranges from -1 to 1
    ssim_score = ssim(output_image, ground_truth_image, multichannel=True)
    
    # Convert SSIM score to the range [0, 1]
    # Since SSIM ranges from -1 to 1, we add 1 to shift the range to [0, 2]
    # Then we divide by 2 to get the score in the range [0, 1]
    ssim_score_normalized = (ssim_score + 1) / 2
    
    # Ensure the result is within [0, 1] range
    ssim_score_normalized = max(0, min(ssim_score_normalized, 1))
    
    return ssim_score_normalized

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

def task_func(x, y, labels):
    # Combine x and y into a single dataset
    data = np.column_stack((x, y))
    
    # Perform PCA
    pca = PCA(n_components=2)
    pca_results = pca.fit_transform(data)

    # Create a scatter plot of the PCA results
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    scatter = ax.scatter(pca_results[:, 0], pca_results[:, 1], c=np.arange(len(labels)), cmap='viridis')
    
    # Label each point
    for i, label in enumerate(labels):
        ax.annotate(label, (pca_results[i, 0], pca_results[i, 1]))
    
    ax.set_title('PCA Results')
    ax.set_xlabel('Principal Component 1')
    ax.set_ylabel('Principal Component 2')
    plt.colorbar(scatter)
    
    return (pca_results, fig)

def explained_variance_ratio(output, ground_truth):
    # Extract PCA results
    pca_results = output[0]
    pca_results_gt = ground_truth[0]
    
    from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
    
    # Calculate MSE between PCA results
    mse = mean_squared_error(pca_results_gt, pca_results)
    
    # Assuming that the maximum possible MSE is 1 (this might need to be adjusted based on the actual data)
    max_possible_mse = 1.0
    
    # Normalize MSE to be in the range [0, 1], where 1 is the best
    # We subtract the normalized MSE from 1 to invert the scale (lower MSE is better)
    normalized_mse = 1 - (mse / max_possible_mse)
    
    # Ensure the result is within [0, 1] range
    normalized_mse = max(0, min(normalized_mse, 1))
    
    return normalized_mse

import statistics
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def task_func(sales_data):
    months = list(range(1, 13))  # Represents the months from January to December
    products = ['Product A', 'Product B', 'Product C', 'Product D', 'Product E']
    
    # Prepare data for plotting
    sales_means = [statistics.mean(sales_data[product]) for product in products]
    sales_stdevs = [statistics.stdev(sales_data[product]) for product in products]

    # Create the plot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    
    # Plotting each product's sales trend
    for i, product in enumerate(products):
        ax.plot(months, sales_data[product], label=product)
        ax.fill_between(months, 
                        [s - sales_stdevs[i] for s in sales_data[product]], 
                        [s + sales_stdevs[i] for s in sales_data[product]], 
                        alpha=0.1)

    # Adding labels and title
    ax.set_xlabel('Month')
    ax.set_ylabel('Sales')
    ax.set_title('Sales Trends for Five Products Over a Year')
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.set_xticklabels([f'Month {m}' for m in months])
    ax.legend()
    
    return (ax,)

import pandas as pd
from scipy.stats import linregress

def task_func(df):
    # Ensure the DataFrame has exactly two columns
    if df.shape[1] != 2:
        raise ValueError("DataFrame must contain exactly two columns.")
    
    # Extract the two variables
    x = df.iloc[:, 0]
    y = df.iloc[:, 1]
    
    # Perform linear regression
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = linregress(x, y)
    
    # Calculate predicted values
    df['predicted'] = slope * x + intercept
    
    return (df,)

def r_squared(output, ground_truth):
    import numpy as np    
    # 提取预测值和真实值
    output=output[0]
    ground_truth=ground_truth[0]
    predicted = output.iloc[:, 1]
    true_values = ground_truth.iloc[:, 1]
    
    # 计算总平方和（Total Sum of Squares, TSS）
    mean_true = np.mean(true_values)
    tss = np.sum((true_values - mean_true) ** 2)
    
    # 计算回归平方和（Regression Sum of Squares, RSS）
    rss = np.sum((predicted - true_values) ** 2)
    
    # 计算决定系数 R²
    r_squared = 1 - (rss / tss)
    
    return r_squared

def dataframe_completeness(output, ground_truth):

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

def task_func(df, target):
    # Splitting the data into features and target
    X = df.drop(columns=[target])
    y = df[target]

    # Creating and training the Linear Regression model
    model = LinearRegression()
    model.fit(X, y)

    # Making predictions
    y_pred = model.predict(X)

    # Calculating the R-squared score
    score = r2_score(y, y_pred)

    return (score, model)

def r_squared_score(output, ground_truth):
    score_output = output[0]  # extract the R-squared score from tuple
    score_ground_truth = ground_truth[0]
    return abs(score_output - score_ground_truth) < 1e-4  # check if scores are approximately equal

import pandas as pd
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

def task_func(df):
    pca = PCA(n_components=2)
    principal_components = pca.fit_transform(df)
    df_pca = pd.DataFrame(data=principal_components, columns=['PC1', 'PC2'])
    explained_var = pca.explained_variance_ratio_.tolist()
    return df_pca, explained_var

def task_func(L):

import unittest
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

class TestCases(unittest.TestCase):

    def lin_relation_1d(self, x, w0, w1):
        '''1-d linear relation for testing'''
        return w0 + w1 * x

    def lin_relation_nd(self, row, w0, w):
        '''n-dimension linear relation for testing'''
        result = 0
        for i, x in enumerate(row.values):
            result += x * w[i]
        return w0 + result 

    def test_case_df(self):
        '''non DataFrame input'''
        df = 3
        target_column = 'test'
        self.assertRaises(Exception, task_func, df, target_column)

    def test_case_target_column(self):
        '''target column not in DataFrame'''
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=0)
        df = pd.DataFrame(rng.integers(0, 10, size=(5, 2)), columns=['test', 'python'])
        target_column = 'not'
        self.assertRaises(Exception, task_func, df, target_column)

    def test_case_empty_df(self):
        '''empty df as input'''
        df = pd.DataFrame(columns=['A', 'B'])
        target_column = 'A'
        self.assertRaises(Exception, task_func, df, target_column)

    def test_case_non_numeric_values(self):
        '''df not numeric'''
        data = {
            'A': [1, 2, 'test'],
            'B': [3, 3, 3]
        }
        df = pd.DataFrame(data)
        target_column = 'A'
        self.assertRaises(Exception, task_func, df, target_column)

    def test_case_1(self):
        '''prediction for one column'''
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=0)
        df = pd.DataFrame(rng.integers(0, 100, size=(1000, 1)), columns=list('A'))
        df['predict'] = df.apply(self.lin_relation_1d, args=(2, 4))
        model = task_func(df, 'predict')
        self.assertIsInstance(model, LinearRegression, "Returned value is not a LinearRegression model.")
        # make sure predictions work as expected
        pred = model.predict(df.drop('predict', axis=1))
        self.assertTrue(np.allclose(pred.tolist(), df['predict'].tolist()))
        # assert model params
        self.assertAlmostEqual(model.coef_[0], 4, places=4)
        self.assertAlmostEqual(model.intercept_, 2, places=4)

    def test_case_2(self):
        '''multiple column prediction'''
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=0)
        df = pd.DataFrame(rng.integers(0, 100, size=(1000, 5)), columns=list('ABCDE'))
        df['predict'] = df.apply(self.lin_relation_nd, axis=1, args=(4, [2.5, 5.8, 6, 4, -1]))
        model = task_func(df, 'predict')
        self.assertIsInstance(model, LinearRegression, "Returned value is not a LinearRegression model.")
        # make sure predictions work as expected
        pred = model.predict(df.drop('predict', axis=1))
        self.assertTrue(np.allclose(pred.tolist(), df['predict'].tolist()))
        # assert model params
        self.assertTrue(np.allclose(model.coef_, [2.5, 5.8, 6, 4, -1]))
        self.assertAlmostEqual(model.intercept_, 4, places=4)

    def test_case_3(self):
        '''test working target value --> with target value linear regression can't deliver good results'''
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=0)
        df = pd.DataFrame(rng.integers(0, 10, size=(1000, 1)), columns=list('A'))
        df['predict'] = df.apply(self.lin_relation_1d, args=(0, 2))
        model = task_func(df, 'predict', target_values=[1, 2, 4, 8])
        self.assertIsInstance(model, LinearRegression, "Returned value is not a LinearRegression model.")

        # make sure predictions work as expected
        masked_df = df.applymap(lambda x: x if x in [1, 2, 4, 8] else 0)
        masked_predict = masked_df['predict']
        pred = model.predict(masked_df.drop('predict', axis=1))
        self.assertTrue(not np.allclose(pred.tolist(), masked_predict.tolist()))
        # assert model params
        self.assertAlmostEqual(model.coef_[0], 0.2921456, places=2)
        self.assertAlmostEqual(model.intercept_, 0.81175, places=4)

    def test_case_4(self):
        '''df with constant values'''
        df = pd.DataFrame(np.full((10, 10), 3), columns=list('ABCDEFGHIJ'))
        model = task_func(df, 'J')
        self.assertTrue(all(coef == 0 for coef in model.coef_), "Model coefficients are not correct.")
        self.assertAlmostEqual(model.intercept_, 3, places=4)

    def test_case_5(self):
        '''df filled with random floats'''
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=0)
        df = pd.DataFrame(rng.random(size=(1000, 5)) * 10, columns=list('ABCDE'))
        df['predict'] = df.apply(self.lin_relation_nd, axis=1, args=(-1, [15, -4.8, 12, 40.2, -2]))
        model = task_func(df, 'predict')
        self.assertIsInstance(model, LinearRegression, "Returned value is not a LinearRegression model.")

import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm

def task_func(df: pd.DataFrame, height: int, weight: int, columns: list) -> tuple:
    if df.empty:
        return None, False
    
    filtered_df = df[(df[columns[1]] > height) & (df[columns[2]] < weight)]
    
    if filtered_df.empty:
        return None, False
    
    X = filtered_df[columns[1:]]
    y = filtered_df[columns[0]]
    
    X = sm.add_constant(X)  # Adds a constant term to the predictor
    
    model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()
    return model, True

import pandas as pd
import heapq
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

def task_func(df, feature, target, n=10):
    if feature not in df.columns or target not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError("Specified columns are not in the provided DataFrame.")
    
    X = df[[feature]].values
    y = df[target].values
    
    model = LinearRegression()
    model.fit(X, y)
    
    predictions = model.predict(X)
    residuals = y - predictions
    
    # Get the indices of the n largest residuals
    largest_residuals_indices = heapq.nlargest(n, range(len(residuals)), key=lambda i: abs(residuals[i]))
    
    return (largest_residuals_indices, model)

import time
import numpy as np

def task_func(samples=10, delay=0.1):
    delays = []

        start_time = time.time()
        time.sleep(delay)
        end_time = time.time()
        actual_delay = end_time - start_time
        delays.append(actual_delay)

import re
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter
from Levenshtein import ratio

# Constants
ALPHANUMERIC = re.compile('[\W_]+')

def clean_text(text):
    cleaned_text = ALPHANUMERIC.sub(' ', text).lower()
    return cleaned_text.strip()

def cosine_similarity(text1, text2):
    words1 = text1.split()
    words2 = text2.split()
    
    vector1 = Counter(words1)
    vector2 = Counter(words2)
    
    intersection = set(vector1) & set(vector2)
    numerator = sum(vector1[word] * vector2[word] for word in intersection)
    
    sum1 = sum(vector1[word] ** 2 for word in vector1)
    sum2 = sum(vector2[word] ** 2 for word in vector2)
    denominator = np.sqrt(sum1) * np.sqrt(sum2)
    
    if not denominator:
        return 0.0
    else:
        return numerator / denominator

def task_func(text1, text2):
    cleaned_text1 = clean_text(text1)
    cleaned_text2 = clean_text(text2)
    
    cosine_sim = cosine_similarity(cleaned_text1, cleaned_text2)
    levenshtein_r = ratio(cleaned_text1, cleaned_text2)
    
    return (cosine_sim, levenshtein_r)
